rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical dicarboxylation of styrene with CO2 at a sacrificial Mg anode to give 2-phenylsuccinic acid
electrochemical_role: Cathodic one-electron reduction of styrene to its radical anion; sacrificial Mg anode supplies Mg2+ to stabilize carboxylate intermediates
electron_flow: Cathode delivers one electron to styrene forming a radical anion B; B attacks CO2 yielding the monocarboxylated radical C, which is further reduced at the cathode to the benzyl carbanion D; D attacks a second CO2 to give the dicarboxylate E. Mg anode is oxidized to Mg2+, which pairs with the carboxylates.
rate_determining_step: Heterogeneous one-electron transfer to styrene (α = 0.21, ∂Ep/∂lg v ≈ 70.7 mV/decade, indicating slow heterogeneous electron transfer)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of styrene with/without CO2
probe_reagents.observation: Without CO2: irreversible peak at -2.64 V (vs SCE). With 0.28 mol/L CO2: original peak shifts to less negative potentials and a new reduction peak appears at more negative potentials, supporting sequential SET/CO2 addition pathway.

notes: Mechanism is supported primarily by CV data and consistent with general electrocarboxylation literature; no radical traps or isotope labeling were employed. The Mg2+ generated at the sacrificial anode is invoked to stabilize/precipitate the carboxylate intermediates and shift equilibria toward product. Lower temperatures favor carboxylation by increasing CO2 solubility.
